reasons.bucket_open: colonial politics / racial discrimination in Rhodesia
reasons.rationale: The speaker discusses political rights and discrimination in Rhodesia in the context of African political rights and representation, not women's political rights.

quotes.text: El Dorado of industrial exploitation and political discrimination
I am not one of those who say that one man, one vote, is the solution

top_quote.text: El Dorado of industrial exploitation and political discrimination

reasons.rationale: The speech is entirely about industrial relations legislation, redundancy provisions, and employment law, with no reference to women's political rights or representation.

quotes.text: I welcome any Bill that seeks to improve industrial relations practice
Part IV, dealing with redundancies

top_quote.text: I welcome any Bill that seeks to improve industrial relations practice

reasons.rationale: The TARGET speech is about government training schemes for unemployed women as a labour/social policy matter, not about women's political rights or representation.

quotes.text: the training of unemployed women and young persons
34,000 women had been trained by the end of 1926

top_quote.text: the training of unemployed women and young persons

reasons.rationale: The speaker argues that Jewish councils expressed willingness to let women vote on the same terms as men, implying women deserve equal political rights.

quotes.text: all the Jewish councils...expressed their willingness to see women vote on the same terms as men

reasons.rationale: The speaker uses the precedent of Muslim women enjoying political rights in Turkey and India to argue that denying women in Palestine political rights is unjustifiable.

quotes.text: in Turkey and in India Moslem women are permitted to enjoy political rights

reasons.rationale: The context shows the questioner pressing the government to extend women's voting rights at least to the level of Oriental countries, using comparative governance as a pragmatic argument.

quotes.text: extend the voting rights of women so as to bring them at least up to the level of those in Oriental countries

top_quote.text: in Turkey and in India Moslem women are permitted to enjoy political rights

reasons.rationale: The speaker cites women voting for the first time in Yugoslavia's history as evidence of democratic progress, framing women's enfranchisement as a positive democratic achievement.

quotes.text: for the first time in the history of Yugoslavia, the women exercised the right to vote
one woman over 100 years old, a peasant, said she had lived her life to see the day when she could exercise the right to vote

reasons.rationale: The speaker uses the expansion of the electorate (including women) as pragmatic evidence that democracy and freedom exist in Yugoslavia, countering claims of totalitarianism.

quotes.text: the number of the electorate was raised from 3,829,000 to 8,020,000
Would one suggest, with figures of that description, that democracy was not being promoted?

top_quote.text: for the first time in the history of Yugoslavia, the women exercised the right to vote

reasons.rationale: The speaker discusses the treatment of married women under a National Health Insurance Bill, focusing on sickness benefit rates and whether married women malinger more than men, which is a social insurance policy debate rather than a political rights or representation debate.

quotes.text: there is a provision in the Bill for the cutting down of the benefit of married women
the implication 1976 which is being drawn by the general public that women in general, and married women in particular, are malingerers

top_quote.text: the implication 1976 which is being drawn by the general public that women in general, and married women in particular, are malingerers

reasons.rationale: The speaker argues for women's (mothers') right to receive family allowances directly, framing it as a matter of fair recognition of women's contributions and opposing a bill that would endow fatherhood instead.

quotes.text: it was really a Bill which would in fact be an endowment of fatherhood
whether the mother should receive the allowance

reasons.rationale: The speaker highlights the practical value of women's unpaid domestic labour, arguing it should be recognized economically, implying women deserve equal treatment in financial policy affecting the home.

quotes.text: the working housewife in the home gives all these services to the home
assess the services of the wife in the home in terms of money

reasons.rationale: The speaker notes the imbalance that 601 male Members will vote to determine an outcome affecting women, implicitly advocating for greater women's representation in political decision-making.

quotes.text: 601 men in the House will determine whether the mother should receive the allowance
the women Member are very generous in welcoming a free vote

top_quote.text: 601 men in the House will determine whether the mother should receive the allowance

reasons.rationale: The speaker argues that women's roles are parallel but not identical to men's, opposing their entry into professions like medicine, law, and by extension wider political/public life.

quotes.text: women should be put on an absolute equality with men
the career of women was not identical with, but parallel with that of the men

reasons.rationale: The speaker appeals to natural temperament and gender roles, arguing women are better suited to nursing than to professional equality with men.

quotes.text: admirably suited to the gentle patient temperament of the female sex
make one of those she-doctors elbowing their way in the world with a masculine activity

reasons.rationale: The speaker warns that the Bill is a pretext to establish a broader precedent for women's equality across all professions and public life throughout the United Kingdom.

quotes.text: snatch a precedent from Parliament on a matter in which the whole of the United Kingdom was concerned
whether women should graduate in every branch of art and science, and follow any profession

top_quote.text: the career of women was not identical with, but parallel with that of the men

reasons.rationale: The speaker argues strongly for equal representation of women in the Scottish Parliament, citing the 50:50 goal and criticising the Liberal Democrats for failing to commit to gender equality.

quotes.text: we must do all we can to achieve equal representation in our 21st-century Scottish Parliament
the principle that there should be an equal number of men and women as members of the first Scottish Parliament

reasons.rationale: The speaker invokes the suffragette legacy and the 70th anniversary of the Equal Franchise Act to ground the case for women's political representation in historical struggle.

quotes.text: It is 70 years ago this month that the House debated the Second Reading of the Representation of the People (Equal Franchise) Bill
They made it possible for us women to be here today

reasons.rationale: The speaker argues that without positive action by parties, the new Scottish Parliament will remain male-dominated, making the case on practical grounds for guaranteed mechanisms to achieve women's representation.

quotes.text: the new Scottish Parliament will not reflect the people who vote for it. It will be a male-dominated body after all
The only hope for women in Scotland lies with the Labour party

top_quote.text: we must do all we can to achieve equal representation in our 21st-century Scottish Parliament

reasons.rationale: The speaker laments the low proportion of women in political representation as a poor reflection, invoking the anniversary of women's suffrage to argue for better representation.

quotes.text: this puny proportion in Human Rights Year, the year, too, of the anniversary of women's suffrage
is a very poor reflection on Welsh womanhood

reasons.rationale: The speaker calls for action to ensure this poor attitude toward women's political participation is not repeated in the future.

quotes.text: try to ensure that this type of attitude is not repeated in the future

top_quote.text: this puny proportion in Human Rights Year, the year, too, of the anniversary of women's suffrage

reasons.rationale: The speech is primarily about trade union and industrial relations legislation in Northern Ireland, not about women's political rights or representation.

quotes.text: anti-union, anti-worker programme that the Government have introduced
part-time workers—who, in the north of Ireland, are almost by definition women—will have their protection substantially weakened

reasons.rationale: The speech is entirely about student grant rates, review processes, and education funding logistics, with no reference to women's political rights or representation.

reasons.rationale: The speaker focuses on the structural problems of unemployment insurance, particularly flat-rate contributions and benefits, comparing British and Continental systems for economic efficiency.

quotes.text: adherence to the British method of flat rates of contributions and flat rates of benefit

reasons.rationale: The TARGET speech is primarily about Post Office revenue and expenditure, with only a passing, neutral reference to woman suffrage as one form of public agitation that could boost postal revenue through increased correspondence.

quotes.text: without expressing any view with regard to woman suffrage
Agitate agitate, so long as you do it in the proper channel

reasons.rationale: The speaker personally supports women having votes for municipal purposes, indicating support for women's political enfranchisement.

reasons.rationale: The speaker explains his restraint from acting on women's suffrage is only due to fears it would endanger the Bill, not opposition to the cause itself.

quotes.text: it was thought by those in charge of it that it would endanger the Bill

top_quote.text: he personally was concerned he was in favour of women having votes for municipal purposes

reasons.rationale: The Home Secretary announces the remission of sentences for suffrage prisoners, treating them on equal footing with strike-related prisoners, recognising their political agitation as worthy of clemency.

quotes.text: remit the remainder of the sentences of all persons now undergoing terms of imprisonment for crimes committed in connection with the suffrage agitation
without solicitation on their part, and without requiring any undertaking from them

reasons.rationale: The statement implicitly validates the suffrage cause by trusting released prisoners not to 'stain the causes they have at heart', acknowledging the legitimacy of suffrage as a cause.

quotes.text: trusted not to stain the causes they have at heart by any further crime or disorder

top_quote.text: remit the remainder of the sentences of all persons now undergoing terms of imprisonment for crimes committed in connection with the suffrage agitation

reasons.rationale: The speaker is debating the Reform Bill regarding parliamentary representation for towns and boroughs, with no reference to women's political rights or representation.

quotes.text: the Bill went far beyond this, and introduced a uniform rate of franchise
it was right and fitting that the great towns of Manchester, and Birmingham, and Leeds, should have conferred upon them the right of returning Representatives

top_quote.text: the Bill went far beyond this, and introduced a uniform rate of franchise

reasons.rationale: The speaker argues that limiting overtime for adult women is unnecessary and harmful, as women themselves have not expressed a desire for such restrictions and are capable of managing their own interests.

quotes.text: the Legislature ought not to go out of its way to limit the independent action of adult women with regard to their hours of work
The clause was inserted to meet the views of certain trades unions which desired to restrict female labour in certain cases

reasons.rationale: The speaker affirms women's competence to decide for themselves, and references their growing voting power as a reason not to restrict their autonomy.

quotes.text: seeing the voting power which was now being given to women
The majority of adult women are quite competent to know what was best in their own interests

reasons.rationale: The speaker argues that restrictions on women's labour were not sought by women themselves, implying respect for their political and personal agency.

quotes.text: If women desired the limitation proposed they would have clearly expressed that view, and they had not done so

top_quote.text: seeing the voting power which was now being given to women

reasons.rationale: The speaker advocates for more women to be elected to parliament both in Tanzania and the UK, referencing the 30% Beijing target for women on elected bodies.

quotes.text: they wanted to know how we planned to meet the 30 per cent. Beijing target for women on elected bodies
The aim is to ensure that more women are involved in politics

reasons.rationale: The speaker highlights a British Council programme supporting women in political leadership, praising its effectiveness in increasing women's representation and encouraging more women to enter politics.

quotes.text: the British Council in east and central Africa has run a programme to support and encourage women in politics
its incredibly good work in increasing the number of women in effective leadership

reasons.rationale: The speaker praises the hard work, dignity, and confidence of women MPs in Tanzania as evidence of their capability and fitness for political office.

quotes.text: the courage, professionalism and sheer hard work of women Members of Parliament in Tanzania

reasons.rationale: The speaker raises the concern that removing the secret ballot could expose women to intimidation by family members, framing the protection of women's free political participation as a key consideration.

quotes.text: people—let us say that they could mainly be women in this context—can vote in safety in the privacy of the ballot booth
they could be intimidated by other family members or who knows whom

reasons.rationale: The speaker explicitly states support for extending voting rights and municipal franchise to women.

quotes.text: strongly in favour of extending the Parliamentary and municipal franchises to women

reasons.rationale: The speaker argues women lack the qualities required to serve as chairman of a District Council, distinguishing voting rights from holding office.

quotes.text: qualities required in a chairman of one of these bodies which few women possessed
exceedingly doubtful question whether a woman ought to hold the office of chairman

reasons.rationale: The speaker draws a sharp distinction between the privilege of voting and the capability of serving on or leading elected bodies, limiting women's political role.

quotes.text: the privilege of voting and the capability of acting on an Elected Body were different things

reasons.rationale: The speaker argues that the current electoral system fails to produce fair representation of women as MEPs, citing Britain's near-worst record in Europe as evidence the system needs reform.

quotes.text: does it ensure that the right proportion of women are elected as MEPs? It does not.
only two countries in Europe—Italy and Portugal—had a lower proportion of women MEPs elected than Britain

reasons.rationale: The speaker uses the poor representation of women as one of several pragmatic failures of the current system to argue for electoral reform that would improve proportionality and fairness.

quotes.text: we do not have other compensating factors
The 829 argument, "If it ain't broke, don't fix it," does not stand up.

reasons.rationale: The speaker argues that Service wives should have equal voting rights as civilian voters or servicemen, highlighting that large numbers of women are being denied the ability to vote.

quotes.text: there are still large numbers of women not able to vote
They want the option either to be treated as civilian voters or as Service men for the purpose of registration

reasons.rationale: The speaker cites practical failures in the registration system that prevented Service wives from exercising their right to vote, calling for procedural reforms.

quotes.text: of that number 178 were not eligible to vote
I should like to see the process of attestation abolished and the "S" deleted. Those are minimum requirements.

reasons.rationale: The speaker supports women reaching Parliament on their own merits and expresses ambition for more women to serve, framing this as genuine equality.

quotes.text: every woman who sits in this House—I sincerely hope that the numbers are set to multiply—knows that she got here on her own merits
It is my ambition that every woman who sits in the House in future will also be able to sit here on her own merits.

reasons.rationale: The speaker opposes positive discrimination (quotas/reserved places) for women in political selection, arguing it is patronising and does not advance women's political standing.

quotes.text: Positive discrimination does not work. All it does is result in is a thoroughly patronising attitude to women.
She was not 497 handed it as a patronising gift from men.

reasons.rationale: The speaker highlights increased childcare provision as freeing women to participate in public life, including work and by extension political participation.

quotes.text: Why are they not delighted at what those increases represent for freeing women to go to work?

reasons.rationale: The TARGET speech is entirely about the complexity and unintelligibility of betterment levy provisions in a Land Bill, with no reference to women's political rights or representation.

every sale of a house, every lease of a house, the grant of a right of way

reasons.rationale: The speaker argues for protecting the interests of women voters and criticises how the House treats women, including ignoring the views of women Members.

whether they die or live

reasons.bucket_open: economic/childcare policy without political rights frame

reasons.rationale: The speaker frames universal suffrage and women's emancipation as major irreversible achievements of the twentieth century that represent progress in democratic practice and equality.

quotes.text: the achievement of universal suffrage. Many people think of the campaign for votes for women

reasons.rationale: The speaker argues these changes are irreversible and that institutions must adapt to accommodate women's new legal rights including property rights in family law.

reasons.rationale: The speaker explicitly accepts the justice of extending the vote to women and identifies as a supporter of women's suffrage.

quotes.text: Everything that has been said by the promoters of the Bill as to the justice of the case made out for extending the vote to women we accept.
a number of Members who sit on these benches on this side of the House who are strongly convinced that the vote ought to be given to women

reasons.rationale: The speaker, while supporting women's suffrage in principle, raises pragmatic concerns that this particular Bill's property qualification provisions could disadvantage the broader cause of enfranchising all women.

only those who are in a position to have more than one house...will be able to take advantage of Clause 2 of the Bill

reasons.rationale: The speaker advocates for the inclusion of women in regional appointments, arguing their knowledge and experience is of great importance.

women who can add the human element which 270 men, in their desire to serve the big economy

the two speeches to which we have just listened have made me doubt the wisdom of my first concession

top_quote.text: The position has been to some extent overstrained by those who—though I do not wish to say anything offensive—have this obsession about women

reasons.rationale: The speaker warns that women having the vote would constitute a 'serious menace,' framing it as a threat to public order and stability.

quotes.text: the serious menace which would be the result if women had a vote

reasons.rationale: The speaker argues that a change of such magnitude should not be made via a private member's bill on a Friday afternoon, but should be decided at a general election, suggesting the gravity of the constitutional change warrants deliberation through established democratic processes.

quotes.text: utterly wrong and indefensible to propose a change of this magnitude in the Bill of a private member, introduced oh a Friday afternoon
it was a subject of sufficient importance to warrant its being discussed in the country at a general election

reasons.rationale: The speaker insists opponents are not motivated by illiberal or ungenerous grounds but by honest convictions and a desire to do what is best for women, framing opposition as morally considered.

quotes.text: they were actuated by a desire to do what was best for women
they were honest convictions

top_quote.text: the serious menace which would be the result if women had a vote

reasons.rationale: The Prime Minister indicates willingness to make time for the Women's Suffrage Bill, describing himself as pushing on an open door, suggesting practical support for advancing the legislation.

quotes.text: the right hon. Gentleman, so far as I am concerned, is pushing an open door
With regard to the Women's Suffrage Bill

top_quote.text: the right hon. Gentleman, so far as I am concerned, is pushing an open door

reasons.rationale: The speaker opposes a Divorce Bill on grounds that it introduces a new principle allowing a guilty party to force an unwilling innocent spouse into divorce, which is unrelated to women's political rights.

quotes.text: it introduces an entirely new principle—that the guilty party should be able to force
divorce which is unpalatable to that spouse

top_quote.text: it introduces an entirely new principle—that the guilty party should be able to force

reasons.rationale: The speaker's primary concern is about employment legislation (Sex Discrimination Bill) affecting teachers and union organization, not women's political rights or representation.

quotes.text: assistant schoolmistresses and assistant masters, if they wish, have an organisation of their own
so much of this legislation gives the ordinary woman the worst of both worlds

top_quote.text: so much of this legislation gives the ordinary woman the worst of both worlds

reasons.rationale: The speaker highlights concrete positive outcomes from using proactive mechanisms to increase women's representation in devolved institutions.

quotes.text: the Welsh Assembly and the Scottish Parliament have some of the best representation of women in Europe
there are more women than men in the Cabinet of the Welsh Assembly

reasons.rationale: The speaker frames devolved parliaments' high female representation as 'great progress', implying women's equal participation in political office is a desirable goal.

quotes.text: great progress has been made in this country in setting up the Welsh Assembly and the Scottish Parliament
A positive mechanism was used by a political party to ensure that

top_quote.text: the Welsh Assembly and the Scottish Parliament have some of the best representation of women in Europe

reasons.rationale: The speech is entirely about British foreign policy toward Italy, Austria, and Piedmont, and criticism of a statesman's conduct as Foreign Secretary — no reference to women's political rights.

quotes.text: his submissive attitude to Austria is not genuine
the same morbid desire for active interference in Italy

top_quote.text: his submissive attitude to Austria is not genuine

reasons.rationale: The speaker is arguing for extending compulsory military service (conscription) to Ireland, not about women's political rights or representation.

quotes.text: no further demands should be made upon the man-power of Great Britain without an assurance from the Government of its intention to bring forward a concurrent measure for compulsory military service in Ireland
why do we not go over to Ireland and bring in all the men who are knocking about at home doing nothing

top_quote.text: no further demands should be made upon the man-power of Great Britain without an assurance from the Government of its intention to bring forward a concurrent measure for compulsory military service in Ireland

reasons.rationale: The speaker invokes women's historical exclusion from voting and standing for Parliament as evidence that the unreformed House of Lords is a broken institution that must change to reflect modern democratic equality.

quotes.text: no women could vote, let alone stand for Parliament
our country has changed beyond recognition, our society has changed beyond recognition and it is long past time that our Parliament changed too

reasons.rationale: The speaker argues that the Lords' hereditary membership — almost all male and white — lacks representativeness and should be reformed to better reflect the broader population.

quotes.text: Almost all those people are men; almost all are white
750 people are Members of the House of Lords by accident of birth

top_quote.text: no women could vote, let alone stand for Parliament

reasons.rationale: The speaker argues that the government's aid cuts drop the unique focus on women, who are among the most vulnerable populations, and criticises this as a failure of stated government aims to promote women's status.

quotes.text: It drops the unique focus on women, yet women and their children make up 80 per cent. of all refugees
promote the status of women and to help to tackle environmental problems

reasons.rationale: The speaker argues that removing focus on women in aid policy is counterproductive given that women and girls bear the heaviest burdens of poverty and illiteracy in the poorest societies.

quotes.text: in the poorest societies women and girls bear the heaviest burdens of illiteracy and hunger
The Government's aim to help the poor—especially women—has wide public support

top_quote.text: It drops the unique focus on women, yet women and their children make up 80 per cent. of all refugees

reasons.bucket_open: Northern Ireland political process / party exclusion
reasons.rationale: The TARGET speech is about the DUP being excluded from Hillsborough talks and denied access to documentation regarding Northern Ireland governance, with a passing reference to the Women's Coalition as a smaller party that received access.

quotes.text: the Women's Coalition and the Alliance party—have had the right of access to the documentation

top_quote.text: the Women's Coalition and the Alliance party—have had the right of access to the documentation

reasons.rationale: The TARGET speech focuses primarily on women's health services, NHS spending, cervical and breast cancer screening, and nursery provision in Scotland — social policy topics without a political rights or representation frame.

quotes.text: the Government have increased spending on the National Health Service in Scotland by 20 per cent
there is complete inequality of provision in certain areas and especially in my constituency

top_quote.text: the Government have increased spending on the National Health Service in Scotland by 20 per cent

reasons.rationale: The speaker defends the moral conduct of suffragettes, arguing they patriotically suspended their campaign during wartime, demonstrating virtue and loyalty rather than extremism.

quotes.text: the moment it came to war and deadly danger, they put aside their case and rallied to the Government's support
the militant and nonmilitant suffragettes attacked the Government with all their force

reasons.rationale: The speaker explicitly pushes back against an unfavourable analogy being drawn about the suffrage movement, defending the legitimacy and character of both militant and non-militant suffragettes.

quotes.text: Is he not rather hard on his colleagues in the militant suffrage movement

top_quote.text: the moment it came to war and deadly danger, they put aside their case and rallied to the Government's support

reasons.rationale: The speaker argues against sending the electoral reform bill to a Grand Committee on grounds of wasted parliamentary time and confused legislative machinery, not on grounds of women's political rights.

quotes.text: a great waste of Parliamentary energy and a great waste of Parliamentary time
confounding and confusing our whole legislative machinery

top_quote.text: a great waste of Parliamentary energy and a great waste of Parliamentary time

reasons.rationale: The speaker argues strongly for equal pay and equal rights for women workers, condemning opponents of equal pay as being against equality of opportunity.

quotes.text: against the principle of equal pay for equal work and equality of opportunity for women in industry
totally opposed to any idea that women should have equal rights with their fellow workers

reasons.rationale: The speaker dismisses arguments that women have lesser abilities than men as ludicrous and outdated.

quotes.text: To suggest that women have not the same abilities as men is ludicrous
It is an argument that I thought went out with t

reasons.rationale: The speaker argues the Bill will help women demand at least equality with the lowest-paid male worker, and calls it a scandal that women earn far less than men doing comparable work.

quotes.text: It would be a great incentive to those women to know that they should not be receiving any rate lower than that of any low-paid male worker
It is an absolute scandal that any employer in the country today should pay such low wages

top_quote.text: To suggest that women have not the same abilities as men is ludicrous

reasons.rationale: The speaker affirms women's full rights to be ordained and denies Parliament's right to prevent that, framing it as a matter of rights and justice.

quotes.text: I am fully committed to women's right to be priests and bishops in the Church of England.
I deny Parliament's right to prevent that from happening.

reasons.rationale: The speaker draws on the historical precedent of the suffragettes and other rights movements to argue that active struggle, not pleading, is how women's gains are achieved.

quotes.text: Women who want that right must follow the course of the suffragettes and others who fought for their rights.
all gains by women and all gains of progress in religious freedom have been achieved when people did something

reasons.rationale: The speaker criticises the Bill as entrenching a barrier to further progress for women, suggesting it is counterproductive to women's full rights.

quotes.text: it represents a tiny step forward, although it is also an entrenchment of a law designed to see that that step cannot lead further.

top_quote.text: I am fully committed to women's right to be priests and bishops in the Church of England.

reasons.rationale: The speaker defends the enfranchisement of women ('flapper vote') against Tory critics, asserting belief in democracy and women's right to vote.

quotes.text: I believe in democracy, and in the so-called flapper vote
members of which a short time ago were denouncing their leader for according the "flapper" vote

reasons.rationale: The speaker argues for electoral reform, implicitly connecting the distorted parliamentary representation to a flawed system that undermines fair political participation including women's.

quotes.text: there is something very wrong with the mechanics of our electoral system
this Parliament is not a true representation, but an immense distortio

top_quote.text: I believe in democracy, and in the so-called flapper vote

reasons.rationale: The speaker is arguing about unemployment benefit eligibility for workers locked out during trade disputes, not about women's political rights or representation.

quotes.text: my Amendment is an attempt to remove those hardships
not to give strike benefit to men or women on strike

top_quote.text: my Amendment is an attempt to remove those hardships

reasons.rationale: The speaker argues that both suffragists and anti-suffragists agree women should serve on local authorities, and that local authorities failing to co-opt women is unacceptable.

quotes.text: both the Suffragists and the anti-Suffragists have accepted the principle that women should be on local authorities
The London County Council have not co-opted a single woman, although there have been a great number of vacancies

reasons.rationale: The speaker highlights the practical urgency of having women on local authorities given upcoming major education reforms requiring proper democratic representation.

quotes.text: a certain number of women should be on these local authorities
we are going to run the risk that local authorities who are to have the spending of these large sums of money may not be elected till March, 1919

top_quote.text: both the Suffragists and the anti-Suffragists have accepted the principle that women should be on local authorities
I shall have been in the House 30 years; I entered it on Guy Fawkes day, 1968

reasons.rationale: The speaker mocks the use of tradition/stability arguments against constitutional change by comparing the Prime Minister's rhetoric to that of die-hard Tories who opposed women's suffrage, implying such arguments are discredited.

quotes.text: die-hard Tory opposing votes for women and the suffragettes to the very last
it would destroy in years what has been built up in centuries

reasons.rationale: By drawing a parallel between the Prime Minister's conservative rhetoric and historical opposition to women's suffrage, the speaker implicitly endorses the cause of women's political rights as having been right against such opponents.

quotes.text: Constitutional change … would endanger the very fabric of Britain

top_quote.text: die-hard Tory opposing votes for women and the suffragettes to the very last

reasons.rationale: The speech is entirely about Air Force estimates, defence policy, and criticism of Socialist ministers — not about women's political rights or representation.

quotes.text: Parliament is asked to vote £173 million and 325,000 men and women to this force.

top_quote.text: Parliament is asked to vote £173 million and 325,000 men and women to this force.

reasons.rationale: The TARGET speech concerns supplementary benefits legislation, cohabitation rules, one-parent families, mobility allowances, and industrial injuries benefits — social welfare policy with no political rights or representation frame.

quotes.text: the change in the cohabitation rule that will be effected by one of the later clauses
there are now 700,000 one-parent families in real need

top_quote.text: the change in the cohabitation rule that will be effected by one of the later clauses

reasons.rationale: The speaker argues for including contraception research in permitted embryo research, framing it as important to women, but this is a health/reproductive policy debate, not a debate about women's political rights or representation.

quotes.text: What are women to make of this House if we say that contraception is not sufficiently important
The battle for safe, effective and convenient contraception has not yet been won

reasons.rationale: The speaker argues about funding mechanisms (block grants vs. percentage grants) for health services including maternity and child welfare, not about women's political rights or representation.

quotes.text: percentage Exchequer grants-in-aid on health services
A block grant is quite a different thing from a fixed grant

top_quote.text: A block grant is quite a different thing from a fixed grant

reasons.rationale: The speaker argues the immigration rules formally and deliberately discriminate against women by denying women settled in Britain the same right as men to bring their foreign national spouses into the country.

quotes.text: the rules discriminate against women. The discrimination is formal, intentional and undeniable.
fundamental distinction between the treatment of women resident in this country and that of men who enjoy the same condition

reasons.rationale: The speaker champions the right of all British women, whether by birth or registration, to bring their husbands to the country, criticising the limitation imposed on that right as keeping genuine married couples separated.

quotes.text: many partners in genuine marriages will be kept separate
all British women, irrespective of how they became British, whether by birth or by registration, shall be granted the right to bring their husbands into the country

reasons.rationale: The speaker argues the Home Secretary's concession on women's rights is cosmetic, designed only to placate the European Court of Human Rights while satisfying Conservative backbenchers, rather than genuinely remedying the discriminatory rules.

quotes.text: the Home Secretary will be able to proclaim his liberalism to the European Court of Human Rights
the compact that has been achieved between the Home Secretary and his Back Benchers and that has been enshrined in the regulations

top_quote.text: the rules discriminate against women. The discrimination is formal, intentional and undeniable.

reasons.rationale: The speaker argues for extending polling hours to 10pm to maximize voter turnout, mentioning women incidentally as part of family voting habits, but the core argument is about polling hours legislation, not women's political rights or representation.

quotes.text: An election should not be about limiting people's opportunity to vote
It should expand the opportunities to vote within sensible limits

top_quote.text: An election should not be about limiting people's opportunity to vote

reasons.rationale: The speaker confirms that women will be included in the plebiscite on the basis of universal suffrage, affirming their political enfranchisement.

quotes.text: women will be included in the plebiscite on the basis of universal suffrage
there is to be a new registration this year

top_quote.text: women will be included in the plebiscite on the basis of universal suffrage

reasons.rationale: The speaker argues for genuinely equal opportunities for men and women, opposing gender quotas as contrary to merit-based selection and equal treatment.

quotes.text: there must be genuinely equal opportunities for men and women
selected on merit, and not simply to fulfil the gender quota

reasons.rationale: The speaker credits the previous Conservative government's policies (Opportunity 2000, flexible labour markets) with increasing women's participation in public life, professions, and politics.

quotes.text: Opportunity 2000, which has put more women into public life
More work opportunities for women arise today as a result of the previous Government's commitment to flexible labour markets

reasons.rationale: The speaker references the industrial tribunal ruling that all-women shortlists breached the Sex Discrimination Act and Treaty of Rome, arguing against such quotas as unlawful.

quotes.text: such lists were found to be clearly in breach of the treaty of Rome and the Sex Discrimination Act 1975

top_quote.text: there must be genuinely equal opportunities for men and women

reasons.rationale: The speaker cites a precedent from the Franchise and Registration Bill where he ruled that introducing women would completely alter the character of the Bill, implying women's inclusion was too radical a change to be accommodated.

quotes.text: the introduction of women would completely alter the whole character of the Bill
the Franchise and Registration Bill and the introduction of women into it

top_quote.text: the introduction of women would completely alter the whole character of the Bill

reasons.rationale: The speaker argues that women have rendered effective service in the war effort and therefore have a legitimate claim to political voice, especially on post-war reconstruction questions affecting their interests.

quotes.text: the women of this country have rendered as effective service in the prosecution of the War as any other class of the community
have not the women a special claim to be heard on the many questions which will arise directly affecting their interests

reasons.rationale: The speaker acknowledges that if new franchise qualifications based on State service are introduced, women cannot be excluded because their contribution is equivalent to that of men.

quotes.text: they point out—and we cannot possibly deny their claim
I say quite frankly that I cannot deny that claim

reasons.rationale: Despite acknowledging women's claim, the speaker argues against opening franchise questions during wartime, warning that debating enfranchisement would distract from the war effort.

quotes.text: nothing more fatal to the concentration of the national effort, than that the floodgates should be opened on all those vast complicated questions of the franchise
we should be diverted from that which ought to be our supreme and sole purpose

top_quote.text: the women of this country have rendered as effective service in the prosecution of the War as any other class of the community

reasons.rationale: The TARGET speech is entirely about economic policy, government taxation, purchasing power, and alleged hypocrisy of the ruling party regarding living standards — not about women's political rights or representation.

reasons.rationale: The speech is entirely focused on a Royal Commission Report on mental health, discussing hospital beds, certification, and treatment methods, with no connection to women's political rights or representation.

the importance of care rather than custody, and the removal of all formalities of admission

reasons.rationale: The speaker is arguing against a government decision to withdraw young women (born 1923) from pharmacy work, but the argument is entirely about maintaining healthcare/pharmaceutical services during wartime, not about women's political rights or representation.

reasons.rationale: The speaker argues that repressive measures against militant suffragists would backfire by encouraging the movement, and that the constitutional supporters of women's suffrage are already gaining ground — implying the cause deserves space to progress.

quotes.text: constitutional supporters of Women suffrage are greatly gaining ground
action which would have one inevitable result only, that of giving a vast encouragement to the movement, which he wishes to suppress

reasons.rationale: The speaker opposes 'panic legislation' aimed at suppressing suffragist activity, arguing that the violent section is already losing public support and funds, making extreme measures both unnecessary and counterproductive.

quotes.text: the violent section of the women are daily losing public support, and, what is more, are daily losing funds
This is essentially an appeal to panic legislation or panic action

reasons.rationale: The speaker frames the Home Secretary's public duty as minimising the agitation rather than inflaming it, implicitly defending the women's movement from heavy-handed state suppression.

quotes.text: whatever his legal powers, his public duty was to minimise this agitation as far as possible
The one thing that the militant suffrage movement desires now is exactly that which the hon. and learned Gentleman has been pressing

reasons.rationale: The TARGET speech is primarily about old age pension policy, economic insecurity, and the need for comprehensive social insurance reform, not about women's political rights or representation.

quotes.text: a comprehensive survey and overhaul of our system of social insurance
the first fairly major Bill dealing with old age pensions

reasons.rationale: The speaker argues it would be unfair to women to deny them employment opportunities by voting against the clause, framing women's access to work as a matter of fairness.

quotes.text: it would be, I feel, unfair to women if by voting against this you keep some of them out of employment

reasons.rationale: The speaker explicitly supports women gaining the vote, expressing hope that by the end of the experimental period young women will be enfranchised and thus have greater political power to demand fair treatment.

quotes.text: by that time, I hope, the young women will be 21, and have the vote
hon. Members will have to listen to them more attentively than now

reasons.rationale: The speaker notes that the Labour party courts the women's vote while trade unions treat women unfairly, implying women need political rights and fair representation to protect their interests.
